questions.text: What was the early name of Gautama Buddha?
questions.explanation: He was born as Prince Siddhartha Gautama in Northern India.

questions.text: At what age did Siddhartha Gautama leave home for enlightenment?
questions.explanation: At twenty-five, he left his royal life to seek the source of suffering.

questions.text: Which of these 'sights' did Buddha NOT see when he went out hunting?
questions.explanation: He saw a sick man, an old man, a corpse, and a monk begging.

questions.text: How long did Buddha wander before he sat under a tree?
questions.explanation: He wandered for seven years before he finally attained enlightenment.

questions.text: The tree under which Buddha attained enlightenment was named:
questions.explanation: He renamed the tree the Bodhi Tree (Tree of Wisdom).

questions.text: Where did Buddha preach his first sermon?
questions.explanation: He delivered his first sermon at the city of Benares (Varanasi).

questions.text: Who was Kisa Gotami?
options: A woman whose only son had died
questions.explanation: She was a grieving mother who sought help to bring her dead son back to life.

questions.text: What did Kisa Gotami carry to her neighbors asking for medicine?
questions.explanation: In her grief, she carried the corpse of her son to all her neighbors.

questions.text: What did the neighbors say about Kisa Gotami's mental state?
questions.explanation: People thought she had gone mad because she was asking for medicine for a dead child.

questions.text: Who directed Kisa Gotami to the Buddha?
questions.explanation: A man told her that he knew a physician who could help—the Sakyamuni (Buddha).

questions.text: What did Buddha ask Kisa Gotami to bring him?
questions.explanation: Buddha asked for mustard seeds as a condition for her son's medicine.

questions.text: What was the condition attached to the mustard seeds?
options: They must come from a house where no one had died
They must be grown by her
questions.explanation: The seed had to be from a house where no child, husband, parent, or friend had ever died.

questions.text: What did Kisa Gotami find out as she went from house to house?
options: Every house had plenty of seeds
In every house, someone had died
No one wanted to help her
She was in the wrong village
questions.explanation: She found that the living are few, but the dead are many.

questions.text: Where did Kisa Gotami sit down after failing to get the seeds?
questions.explanation: Weary and hopeless, she sat down by the wayside watching the lights of the city.

questions.text: What metaphor did Kisa Gotami use while watching the city lights?
options: The flicker and extinguishing of lights is like human lives
questions.explanation: She realized that human lives flicker up and are extinguished again, just like the lights.

questions.text: What did Buddha say about the life of mortals?
options: It is troubled, brief, and combined with pain
questions.explanation: Buddha preached that mortal life is short and full of suffering.

questions.text: To what did Buddha compare the danger of death for mortals?
questions.explanation: Just as ripe fruits are in danger of falling, mortals are in danger of death.

questions.text: Buddha compared human life to which of these earthen vessels?
questions.explanation: All earthen vessels made by the potter end in being broken.

questions.text: According to Buddha, can lamenting or grieving bring peace of mind?
options: No, it makes the pain greater
questions.explanation: Grieving only increases pain and makes the body suffer without bringing back the dead.

questions.text: How can one obtain peace of mind according to the sermon?
options: By drawing out the arrow of lamentation
questions.explanation: One must overcome grief and remove the 'arrow' of complaint and sorrow.

questions.text: What happens to the person who overcomes all sorrow?
options: They become free from sorrow and be blessed
questions.explanation: He who has drawn out the arrow will become free from sorrow.

questions.text: Benares is a city holy because it is situated on the banks of:
questions.explanation: Benares (Varanasi) is one of the holiest cities on the river Ganges.

questions.text: The sermon at Benares deals with which universal truth?
questions.explanation: The core lesson is that death is a natural part of life and cannot be avoided.

questions.text: The phrase 'mortals' refers to:
options: Human beings who are subject to death
questions.explanation: Mortals are living beings who eventually die.

questions.text: What does 'enlightenment' mean in this context?
questions.explanation: Enlightenment is the state of having gained deep spiritual insight.

questions.text: Kisa Gotami's realization made her stop being:
questions.explanation: She realized she was selfish in her grief, thinking only of her own loss while death is common to all.

questions.text: The term 'Sakyamuni' refers to:
questions.explanation: Sakyamuni is a title for the Buddha, meaning 'Sage of the Shakyas'.

questions.text: According to the Buddha, grieving makes the body:
questions.explanation: Lamentation causes physical suffering and pain to the body.

questions.text: One who wants to be blessed must avoid:
questions.explanation: Drawing out the arrow of lamentation is the path to blessing.

questions.text: The story emphasizes that death:
options: Is the same for young and old, wise and foolish
questions.explanation: Buddha says that all are subject to death, regardless of status or age.

questions.text: What is the meaning of 'valley of desolation' in the text?
questions.explanation: It refers to the difficult experience of mourning and extreme sadness.

questions.text: Buddha's father was a:
questions.explanation: He was a prince, suggesting his father was a king (Raja).

questions.text: How many days did Siddhartha stay under the tree before enlightenment?
questions.explanation: He vowed to stay until enlightened, which happened after seven days.

questions.text: Which city is mentioned as the most holy of dipping places?
questions.explanation: Benares is described as the most holy of the dipping places on the Ganges.

questions.text: The mustard seed represents:
options: A lesson in the universality of death
questions.explanation: The seed itself was simple, but finding a house without death was the impossible task.

questions.text: What does Buddha compare death to in terms of 'no one can save'?
options: A father cannot save his son
questions.explanation: He notes that neither fathers nor kinsmen can save those who are dying.

questions.text: Who is 'He' who has drawn out the arrow?
options: A wise person who has overcome grief
questions.explanation: It refers to anyone who has reached a state of peace by letting go of sorrow.

questions.text: The tone of Buddha's sermon is:
questions.explanation: The sermon is delivered with deep empathy for human suffering and clarity of thought.

questions.text: The lesson taught to Kisa Gotami was through:
questions.explanation: Buddha made her realize the truth for herself by sending her on the task for seeds.

questions.text: What is the final goal of the sermon?
options: To help people attain peace of mind and overcome grief
questions.explanation: The sermon aims to guide people towards spiritual peace by accepting death.
